topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee shop hummed with the usual midday bustle, a symphony of clattering mugs and muffled conversations. I watched Liam across the small table, his easy laughter directed at a stranger. When he finally turned, his eyes swept over me, a brief, almost mechanical glance that offered no recognition. A tight knot formed in my chest, a familiar, unpleasant clenching. I deliberately took a slow, deliberate sip of my latte, letting the silence stretch, heavy and accusatory. He didn't even flinch. It was as if I were a piece of background noise, easily filtered out. I pushed my untouched pastry aside, the movement sharp and brittle. "Rough day?" I managed, my voice carefully neutral, a thin veneer over the churning dissatisfaction inside. He just shrugged, a gesture so casual it felt like a deliberate dismissal. I felt a distinct heat rise in my neck, a burning sensation I desperately tried to mask with an air of bored indifference. He truly didn't see me.

[story 2]

I stood by the old oak tree, the setting sun casting long, judgmental shadows across the lawn. My hands were shoved deep into my pockets, my jaw set so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. Liam approached, radiating that infuriatingly uncomplicated warmth he always possessed. He started talking about some recent trip, animated and full of shared memories. I listened, nodding at the right intervals, my expression carefully blank. When he paused, waiting for a reaction, I let a slow, deliberate exhale escape, a sound devoid of warmth. "That sounds… nice," I said, the inflection flat, dripping with something sharp and unyielding. He looked momentarily confused, a flicker of something uncertain crossing his face, before he smoothly moved on. I turned my back to him, focusing instead on the distant horizon, feeling a profound sense of separation. It was a bitter taste in my mouth, this realization: he existed in a separate reality where I was simply absent.

[story 3]

The shared history hung between us like stale smoke in the air. I saw him across the crowded university quad, and the sight of him—so comfortable, so entirely unaware of the shared foundation we’d built—made my stomach twist. I walked toward him, my steps measured and slow, each footfall a deliberate assertion of my presence. "Hey," I started, my tone laced with an exaggerated casualness that felt like sandpaper against my tongue. He looked up, and his eyes met mine for a fraction of a second, then slid away, as if reading a blank page. The air seemed to freeze around us. I watched the slight downturn of his mouth, the subtle shift in his posture that suggested he was politely acknowledging a stranger. A wave of profound irritation washed over me, a physical tightening in my shoulders. I stopped, letting the silence settle, an unspoken judgment hanging heavy between us, communicating everything I felt without uttering a single harsh word.
